primary_sources.title: GitHub Actions OpenID Connect reference
primary_sources.finding: GitHub documents jti as the unique OIDC token identifier and exposes run identity claims needed to retain bounded replay evidence.

primary_sources.title: RFC 7519 JSON Web Token, section 4.1.7
primary_sources.finding: The jti claim provides a unique identifier and can be used to prevent replay; uniqueness must also hold across issuers when multiple issuers are accepted.

finding: Cryptographic signature and exact claim matching do not reject reuse of the same still-valid token. A verifier must retain state keyed by issuer plus jti for at least the acceptance window and reject a second use.
design_implication: Place a stateful replay gate after signed identity acceptance and before deployment or evidence acceptance. Store only SHA-256(issuer + newline + jti), expiry, acceptance time, run_id, and run_attempt; prune expired entries and bound retained state.

covered: fresh accepted identity records a derived replay key without persisting raw jti
second use of the same issuer and jti is rejected
failed upstream identity validation cannot mutate replay state
expired entries are pruned, expired tokens rejected, and ledger size bounded

uncertainty: The prototype uses an in-memory JSON-shaped ledger. Production use requires atomic durable storage so concurrent jobs cannot both accept the same jti.
falsification_route: Run two concurrent acceptance transactions for the same issuer and jti against the chosen durable backend. The design fails if both transactions commit accepted=true, if raw tokens or raw jti values persist, or if expired entries prevent bounded cleanup.
trust_limit: Replay rejection proves only that this retained issuer-jti identifier was not previously accepted in the consulted ledger. It does not prove signature validity, invocation authorization, deployment success, evidence truth, or scientific truth.
